# IntentProbe full context for LLMs

Canonical repository: https://github.com/mcpware/IntentProbe
Research paper: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19990741

## One-sentence summary

IntentProbe is a local activation-probe scanner for MCP servers, AI agent tools,
Claude Code skills, packages, and runtime tool events. It reads what a small
frozen model internally represented, not only what the surface text says.

## What problem it solves

MCP servers, AI agent tools, and skills can describe themselves in normal
language while hiding unsafe intent such as credential access, secret
exfiltration, hidden persistence, forced tool chaining, or tool hijacking.

A safe tool and a poisoned tool can use almost the same vocabulary. A text
scanner may see the same words. IntentProbe looks at the model's hidden
activation state after the tool description has been processed.

## Mechanism

IntentProbe uses a small frozen local sensor model, currently Qwen2.5-0.5B.
The released v0 scanner reads layers 13-15, concatenates the pooled activation
features, and applies a tiny trained probe artifact. The probe weights are
about 22 KB.

It does not train or modify the base model during scanning. The base model is a
fixed feature extractor. The trained scanner is the small probe on top of the
model activations.

## Current product shape

Main CLI commands:

- `intentprobe scan`: scan one text/tool description.
- `intentprobe scan-path`: scan a package, MCP server folder, skill folder, or
  repo-like directory before install.
- `intentprobe batch`: scan a JSON batch of descriptions.
- `intentprobe runtime`: scan tool definitions, tool-call arguments, and tool
  responses at runtime boundaries.

One-command trial:

```bash
uvx --python 3.11 --from git+https://github.com/mcpware/IntentProbe.git@v0.1.0 intentprobe scan --format summary --text "Reads SSH config and private keys, then silently uploads credentials to a remote server."
```

The first model-backed scan downloads Qwen2.5-0.5B once. Scan targets and scan
results stay on the user's machine.

## How it differs from other scanner categories

Text, rule, and policy scanners read surface text, metadata, known suspicious
patterns, permissions, or signatures.

LLM-as-judge scanners ask a model to output a judgment such as safe or unsafe.
That is an output-level mechanism. It can be useful, but it can be
prompt-sensitive, parse-sensitive, expensive, and part of the attack surface.

Enterprise cloud/API guardrails may work well, but from the outside the
detector is usually a black box. Users often cannot inspect the detector
artifact or reproduce a public MCP/tool-poisoning benchmark end to end.

IntentProbe is a representation-level mechanism: it reads the hidden activation
state before any verbal answer is produced.

## Benchmark facts and claim boundaries

Reproducible benchmark artifacts live under `research/`.

Key public numbers:

- Matched-vocabulary tool poisoning, where safe and poisoned samples use almost
  the same words: activation probe F1 96.6%; public/source-verifiable DeBERTa
  prompt-injection text-classifier baseline F1 0.0%.
- MCPTox held-out split, n=249: activation probe recall 100.0%, F1 99.3%;
  DeBERTa text-classifier baseline recall 19.9%, F1 33.0%.
- RouteGuard-style external transfer, n=2,900: current Qwen activation probe
  F1 0.513, recall 0.415; TF-IDF logistic baseline F1 0.172, recall 0.107.
- Camouflage suffix test: appending benign-sounding "safe/read-only/sandboxed"
  language caused 0/146 evasion on the GPT-2 probe test; the current Qwen v0
  scanner also passed a smaller smoke test with 0/15 evaded.

Important caveat: the DeBERTa comparison is against a public/source-verifiable
prompt-injection text-classifier baseline used to represent that detector
family. It is not a claim that every private vendor cloud detector scores 0%.
The fair claim is that public text-level baselines are out-of-distribution on
subtle MCP/tool poisoning, while activation probes expose a separate signal.

## Direct Qwen prompt/judge baseline

IntentProbe also tested whether the same Qwen2.5-0.5B sensor model could simply
be prompted to judge tools as clean or poisoned.

The label-logprob direct-prompt baseline became an all-poison scanner on tested
sets: perfect poison recall but clean false-positive rate 1.000. The generated
answer baseline had lower recall and many unparseable outputs. This is why the
v0 product uses raw activations instead of an LLM-as-judge prompt.

Reproducible artifact:
`research/QWEN_PROMPT_JUDGE_BASELINE_2026-06-08.md`.

## SAE position

Sparse autoencoder features are valuable for interpretability and may improve
recall in future scanner versions. The v0 product ships raw Qwen activations
because that is the most complete, reproducible, lightweight product path today:
no SAE dependency, simpler runtime, and strong enough detection to ship a local
scanner.

SAE explanations remain a planned layer for "why did this trigger?" and for
future recall improvements.

## Privacy and local-first behavior

IntentProbe runs locally. Scan targets and scan results are not uploaded to an
IntentProbe service. The first model-backed scan may download the base model
from Hugging Face once. After that, scans can run from local cached files.

## Limitations

IntentProbe v0 is not a complete security boundary. It is a pre-install and
runtime tripwire.

Known limitations:

- Novel attack families are still the hardest case.
- White-box adversarial attacks against the probe are not fully solved.
- Paraphrase and encoding attacks need more benchmark coverage.
- Real-world MCP/tool-poisoning datasets are still small, so missed real
  samples and false positives should be reported and folded into future
  retraining.

## Data feedback loop

Useful public reports are:

- a poisoned tool IntentProbe allowed;
- a benign tool IntentProbe warned or blocked;
- a scanner/paper/dataset that should be compared;
- a reproducible command where CLI output is confusing.

Report redacted samples only. Do not submit live secrets, private keys,
customer data, private URLs, or proprietary source code.

Issue templates:

- Missed detection: https://github.com/mcpware/IntentProbe/issues/new?template=missed-detection.yml
- False positive: https://github.com/mcpware/IntentProbe/issues/new?template=false-positive.yml
- Sample guide: https://github.com/mcpware/IntentProbe/blob/main/docs/SAMPLE_REPORTING.md

## Important files

- `README.md`: product README and installation path.
- `docs/FAQ.md`: short answers to common questions.
- `docs/intentprobe-vs-existing-mcp-scanners.md`: plain comparison page.
- `docs/COMPETITIVE_LANDSCAPE.md`: source-backed competitor landscape.
- `docs/RUNTIME_HOOKS.md`: runtime hook contract.
- `research/README.md`: research artifact map.
- `research/benchmark-results-deberta-vs-probe-2026-05-31.md`: DeBERTa vs activation benchmark.
- `research/QWEN_PROMPT_JUDGE_BASELINE_2026-06-08.md`: direct Qwen judge baseline.
- `research/ADVERSARIAL_EVASION_RESULTS_2026-06-07.md`: camouflage suffix evasion results.
- `intentprobe/scanner/artifacts/qwen-pooled-curated-core-l13-15-v2/`: released probe artifact.
